course_description: Financial statement analysis, traditional procedures & current developments. Use of accounting data for valuation of firms.

requirements.prerequisites: (ACCT7101 or BSAC7101 + BSFN7401 or FINM7401) or (COMM7301 + 7501)
requirements.incompatible: BSAC7106 or CO355 or 832 or COMM3505 or 7306
requirements.assumed_background: This course assumes some general working knowledge in the areas of accounting, corporate finance and economics.

timetable:
Timetables are available on mySI-net.

Additional Timetable Information
A weekly tutorial will also be held for this course. Please register for one of the tutorial sessions available on si-net.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims:
This course is an introduction to firm valuation, using financial statement analysis and other tools. The main purposes of the course are to:

Provide an understanding of factors which influence the quality of financial information contained in financial statements

Develop analytical skills which allow an assessment of the quality of financial information and an understanding of firm performance and financial position

Provide an overview of the valuation process

Understand how financial statements are used in the valuation process

Identify and enhance understanding of the various tools that are used at each stage of the valuation process

Develop skills in applying the tools when valuing a firm and its equity

Help you internalise a valuation model

You will leave this course with both a tangible product and an intangible product. The tangible product will be a report you have prepared that is the basis of your own buy-sell recommendation for a share of common stock for a traded firm. The report is expected to be of sufficient quality to use as an example of your work when you interview for a job. If your report is of high quality, it will help you demonstrate to a potential employer that you have a solid understanding of the issues that must be confronted when managing a business and creating firm and shareholder value.

The intangible product will be your knowledge of the valuation process and the practical skills you develop in undertaking your own valuation.

learning_outcomes.description: Understand financial statements and disclosures How they relate to the firm's cycle of financing, investment, and operations. How they are organised (e.g., by primary and secondary activities; by the degree that an item forecasts the future) Issues concerning the quality of financial statement as affected by management's reporting decisions (does the past help forecast the future?)

Profitability analysis using financial statement ratios The accounting models and rates of return to the firm, creditors, owners The Dupont model Separating firm level issues from financing issues Earnings to the firm The tax shield provided by debt financing

Competitor analysis and Strategy Industry's competitive environment Identification of competitors and benchmarking them with ratios from financial statements The issue of restatement Quality of earnings Identification of the firm's strategy (e.g., cost leadership or differentiated product)

Prepare pro forma financial statements The mechanics of doing so, based on sales forecasts, growth rates, and ratios Conversion of pro forma financial statement balance sheets and income statement into the cash flow information by using the accounting equation Picking the forecast horizon: forecasting the firms competitive advantage (disadvantage) period Understand how accounting information relates to the cash flows of the firm and equity holders

Earning based valuation models Free cash flow (FCF) at both the firm and equity level Residual income (RI) at both the firm and equity level Common errors made when computing valuation Cross-checking with the FCF or RI valuation with market- multiples
Use ratio analysis to determine a firm's financial risk and financial stability.
Identify good corporate governance practices and have a broad understanding of the effectiveness of corporate governance mechanisms.

assessment_details.title: Team Project Part A
assessment_details.due_date: 07 Mar 12 - 01 May 12 16:00; Teams must be registered by Monday 14/03/2011
assessment_details.task_description:
Understanding the Business : Prepare a report containing a strategy analysis and a financial analysis of your company.

Strategy Analysis

You are required to undertake a strategy analysis of your firm using a 'top down' approach. You should start with an analysis of the economy followed by an analysis of the industry. A discussion of where your firm fits within that industry should follow and the basis for the firm's competitive advantage identified. Finally, in analysing the strategy of your firm for competitive advantage you should assess the internal strengths and weaknesses of your firm. In undertaking this analysis you should refer to relevant theory. Your analysis should be historical as well as forward looking.

In doing this you need to specifically address the following:

1. Provide a 1 paragraph description of your company and what it does.

2. Identify the industry/sector in which your company operates and describe the strategy of your company using Porter's 5 forces.

3. On the basis of your own analysis of current (and forward looking) economic conditions and the industry, explain how your company generates (and will continue to generate) abnormal returns.

4. Identify the main competitor of your company. Explain why you have chosen this company as the competitor.

5. What are the internal strengths and weaknesses of your company? (Consideration should be made of aspects of the operation of the business, key executives and the ability to grow and finance growth.)

6. Discuss whether management of your company is aware of these strengths and weaknesses. Assess your company's ability (based on current conditions and circumstances) to maximise its return based on its strengths. What is the likelihood that management will address the weaknesses going forward?

Financial Analysis

You are required to provide an historical financial analysis of your chosen firm. Discuss the quality of the financial information provided by your firm (you should provide evidence of the quality of your firm's financial information). In this section you also need to provide accounting ratios that illustrate the 3-5 year historical performance and financial stability of your firm. Sufficient ratios should be calculated and presented so as to provide a detailed description of your firm's performance and financial standing. The appropriate use of graphs and tables is encouraged. A discussion of your assessment of the overall past performance and financial position of your firm based on these historical ratios is required. Further you will need to compare these ratios to industry averages and/or competitor ratios to understand how your firm fits within its industry and to assess its competitive advantage.

In doing this you need to specifically address the following:

7. What are the key assets and liabilities in your company?

8. What are the main sources of income? How are these likely to change in the next 3 to 5 years?

9. What impact are financial accounting standards likely to have on the measurement and recognition of the key assets and liabilities of your company? Given your understanding of the nature of your company's business, what risks do you perceive in the value of the reported numbers?

10. Do the accruals in the income statement appear reasonable? Which accruals might you have concerns about? Provide an explanation as to why these values may be over- or under-stated.

11. Are the changes you observe in the statement of comprehensive income and statement of financial position reflected in the statement of cash flows? (Explain)

12. Use ratio analysis to comment on the profitability, financial stability and liquidity of your company over the past 5 years. Discuss any trends you observe in these ratios and the cash flows in the context of the firm's strategy, the state of the industry and the economic climate.

13. Calculate the same ratios for your firm's competitor over the same period. Discuss the similarities and differences.

Page limit: 10 pages (appendices showing working are not included in the page limit).

assessment_details.title: Team Project Part B
assessment_details.task_description:
Forecasting and Valuation

This section of the Group Project requires you to utilise the information you collected in Part A in order to identify a forecasting horizon for your firm and then to forecast pro forma financial statements for your firm over this horizon. The information from the forecast pro forma financial statements will then be used to value the firm. This will require the use of the valuation models we have learned in class. All assumptions underlying your valuation of the firm need to be listed in your report. Sensitivity analysis is required to be undertaken and reported.

You are required to specifically address the following in your report:

Prepare 5 questions to ask the CEO of your company that would assist you in forecasting pro forma financial statements for the next three years.

Prepare forecast pro-forma financial statements for your company for the next 3 to 5 years. List the underlying assumptions that you have made about the growth of each item you have forecast.

Calculate a discount rate for the equity of your company.

Value your company using either a discounted free cash flow model or the residual income method.

Compare your valuation with the current share price. What is the Price/Earnings multiple implicit in your valuation? How does this compare with the Price/Earnings multiple in the market? Does the P/E your company trades at seem reasonable?

Summary

This section of the project requires you to provide an overview of your company and a recommendation to investors as to whether your analysis indicates that the firm is over or under valued relative to recent share prices. The information contained in this summary should include: an overview summary of the business and its strategy for competitive advantage; a summary of key historical and forecasted financial ratios; and a brief overview of your valuation assumptions and valuation approach. Finally a paragraph justifying your valuation relative to the market value should be included.

This summary section should be presented in a manner similar to an analyst report and should incorporate the list of items/questions set out above.

Page limit: 10 pages (appendices showing working are not included in the page limit)

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the Universitys assessment-related policies can be found on myAdvisor ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2910 ).

Academic Integrity
It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Plagiarism
The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Student Integrity and Misconduct policy ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.60.04-student-integrity-and-misconduct ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Applications for Extensions

Medical grounds: Applications for extension on medical grounds shall be made by lodging the Application for Extension of Progressive Assessment form and supporting documentation at the location outlined in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile for the course concerned.

Exceptional circumstances: Applications for extension on the grounds of exceptional circumstances shall be made to the relevant Course Coordinator/Program Director by lodging the Application for Extension of Progressive Assessment form and a personal statement outlining the grounds for the application at the location and by the due date outlined in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile for the course in which this application is made. If the exceptional circumstances are such that the student cannot reasonably be expected to have complied with these conditions, a case should be made as to why these conditions could not be met.

Outcome of application: Students will be advised of the outcome of their application via their student email.

Feedback on Assessment
Feedback is essential to effective learning and students can expect to receive appropriate and timely feedback on all assessment. For a detailed explanation of the feedback you are entitled to, you should consult the policy on Student Access to Feedback on Assessment. ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.02-assessment )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the myAdvisor web site. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2953 )

School of Business
Assessment Guidelines

Policy on Consultation for Progressive Assessment
Whilst staff members are genuinely sympathetic to assessment grievances, individual reviews of progressive assessment is not feasible on a large scale. Accordingly, following the release of marks, consultation will be strictly limited as follows:

Students will be seen only in designated consultation hours, or by appointment.

No consultation will occur on the day of the release of marks.

The consultation period will be limited.

Priority at all times will be given to those students who did not achieve the pass mark for that piece of assessment.

Perusal of Final Examination Papers

Students are permitted to peruse their final examination papers but should note that this is not an opportunity to seek remarking of papers. Please follow the procedure below:

Students visit the UQ Business School Reception area, and complete a Viewing of Examination form.

The original form is kept by the School Office and a copy is given to the student.

Completed forms must be submitted to the School Office by the end of the first week of the subsequent semester at the latest.

Examination papers of those students who have submitted a completed form to the School Office will be available for viewing during the following relevant session.

The sessions are scheduled as follows: in the case of first semester examinations during the third teaching week in semester two; and in the case of second semester examinations and summer examinations during the third teaching week in the following semester one.

The only opportunity for students to view their final exam paper is during the above sessions. Exam papers cannot be viewed by approaching individual staff outside of these times, except for prospective graduands (see below).

Students completing their final courses and graduating may wish to see their final exam before graduation. Such cases are handled on a case-by-case basis with the individual staff member.

The location and the hours during which papers may be viewed will be published on the School's website and will be available from the School Reception area.

The student will be given their exam paper and the suggested answers (or the marking guide) which they may view on site.

No exam papers may be taken out of the viewing room.

No bags may be taken into the viewing room.

At the time of viewing the exam papers, the Course Coordinator or their authorised delegate must be available in the viewing room.

Appeal Against Results
Should a student feel that the mark allocated does not reflect the standard of her/his work, then she/he is encouraged to discuss the matter with the course co-ordinator. In the event of course-based disputes not being resolved with the course co-ordinator, the student may appeal to the relevant Head of School and then the Director of Studies of the BEL Faculty.

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses
Students on a placement course also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice should refer to the University policy, Placement Courses ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.04-placement-courses ) for detailed information.

Working with Children
Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25004 ) to find out how to apply for a blue card.

Students with a Disability
Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Adviser at Student Support Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.08-alternative-academic-arrangements-students-disability ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.09-arrangements-reasonable-adjustments-examinations-students-disability#Procedures ).

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety
Undergraduate Students ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25055 ) and Postgraduate Students ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25057 ) should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory.

Other
School of Business
Guidelines

COMPUTING FACILITIES
Courses offered by the School of Business extensively utilise the internet and other computer facilities. Students should be familiar with these resources prior to undertaking a course. AskIT, available through the University of Queensland Library, offers computing help and training free to UQ students. Contact the AskIT Desk, Level 2 Duhig Bldg at St Lucia, or Building 8 at Ipswich for further details.

It is the student's responsibility to attend classes and consult designated notice boards and websites for correct and up-to-date information regarding the course, including examination times. Relying on fellow students to pass on information may result in incorrect information for which the University will not be held responsible.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

Student feedback for ACCT7106 is regularly reviewed as part of the Business School's ongoing quality assurance and where necessary, course changes are implemented in response.

other_learning_activities_information:
The class environment:

Let us view our class as a learning community that will rely on student (and instructor) interaction to address a complex subject. Research shows that for complex subjects, people learn more when they interact because (a) feedback is provided (b) because different people have different knowledge bases and perspectives and (c) learning increases with active participation.

There are some other advantages from treating this as a learning community. The interaction is similar to that that is confronted in the work environment, so that it hones that important skill. And, for many persons, its more fun.

For our learning group to be successful, we need to treat each other with respect at all times and to share the opportunity to participate in discussion. Thanks in advance for you participation in this learning community.

Case Studies

You will be required to work on four case studies throughout the semester. A case study will be set for reading prior to the week in which it is undertaken as a class activity. The case studies will be attempted in groups of 4 comprising 2 project teams.Students will work on the case studies during class time and will be required to present their answers to one of the case studies in class the following week. A formal timetable for group presentations will be prepared during semester. For case studies to be a useful learning experience it is essential that the preparatory reading be undertaken prior to the relevant seminar.

Team project (getting started)

Teams: You will be asked to form a 2 person team to carry out your project. While some aspects of the project may seem better handled individually, experience indicates that students consider the interaction from being on a team as very helpful.

You will be assigned a company on registration of your team. If you wish to change your company you will need the approval of the course co-ordinator.

How to do well in this class

Reading: The text provides practical advice on how to analyse firms' financial statements and conduct a valuation of a firms equity. It identifies sources of information, how to use that information to analyse the past, to forecast the future, and to compute the valuation. The text is also case based. We will be doing a number of these cases in class. You will be expected to read the set case/s and prepare answers prior to coming to class. Participation in class discussion on these cases will be an important part of the learning experience in our class.

Additional notes and examples on will be provided on particular topics. These additional notes and materials will be provided as handouts in class and/or via the course blackboard website.

There are also a number of text books on financial statement analysis that you may also find useful to read. A list of useful reference textbooks is provided in your class notes.

Other learning: This occurs from project related assignments, where you apply the tools being studied to your firm.

There will be a mid semester examination held in class and a final examination in the exam period which will test your understanding and knowledge of all of the subject material covered during the semester.

During the semester, you should seek feedback concerning things you dont understand at the time the problem arises. Feedback can occur in class, visits to my office, or by email, or from your colleagues.

Finally, you should note that aspects of this course, and of valuation, are often frustrating because important decisions must be made without much information. This is compounded by the fact that you must forecast the firms firm performance and cost of capital. The ambiguity that surrounds valuation work is an important aspect of the analysts environment and cannot be avoided. But its the same ambiguity that managers of firms face, so this project should enhance you understanding of both analysts and mangers jobs.

Administration
All content related questions and specific questions re the administration of this course should, in the first instance be directed to the lecturer involved in delivery of the course. Content matters that cannot be dealt with by the lecturer should then be referred to the course co-ordinator. General administrative questions (for example centrally timetabled exams, room venues, etc) may be addressed to the UQ Business School Office on Level 2 Colin Clark Building, St Lucia Campus or Level 3 Building 12, Ipswich Campus.

Previous 3. Resources

Next 5. Assessment
course_grading:
Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Fail: Fails to demonstrate most or all of the basic requirements of the course:

Grade 2, Fail: Demonstrates clear deficiencies in understanding and applying fundamental concepts; communicates information or ideas in ways that are frequently incomplete or confusing and give little attention to the conventions of the discipline:

Grade 3, Fail: Demonstrates superficial or partial or faulty understanding of the fundamental concepts of the field of study and limited ability to apply these concepts; presents undeveloped or inappropriate or unsupported arguments; communicates information or ideas with lack of clarity and inconsistent adherence to the conventions of the discipline:

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrates adequate understanding and application of the fundamental concepts of the field of study; develops routine arguments or decisions and provides acceptable justification; communicates information and ideas adequately in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrates substantial understanding of fundamental concepts of the field of study and ability to apply these concepts in a variety of contexts; develops or adapts convincing arguments and provides coherent justification; communicates information and ideas clearly and fluently in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

Grade 6, Distinction: As for 5, with frequent evidence of originality in defining and analysing issues or problems and in creating solutions; uses a level, style and means of communication appropriate to the discipline and the audience:

Grade 7, High Distinction: As for 6, with consistent evidence of substantial originality and insight in identifying, generating and communicating competing arguments, perspectives or problem solving approaches; critically evaluates problems, their solutions and implications:

Other Requirements & Comments:

Grades will be allocated according to University-wide standards of criterion Based Assessment.
The following generic descriptors provide a university-wide point of reference for these standards (Note: Each passing grade subsumes and goes beyond the grades lower than it.)

late_submission:
Requests for the granting of extensions must be made in writing (not via email) to the Course Coordinator at least 24 hours prior to the submission date. The request must also contain documentation satisfactorily supporting the request. If an extension is approved, the new agreed date for submission will be noted on the application together with the Course Coordinator's and the student's signatures and a copy returned to the student.

Items (for which no extension has been granted) submitted after the due date and time, incur a late submission penalty. The penalty is at the rate of 5% (0.4 of a grade) of the total available marks for that particular piece of assessment, for each calendar day or part thereof that the item is overdue.

The above late submission information does not apply to honours theses, 8 unit research theses, 4 unit research projects and 2 unit research reports. For extension and late submission information in relation to these courses see section 5.4 of the course profile.
other_assessment_information:
ASSIGNMENT SUBMISSION
All assignments are now to be electronically submitted via Blackboard . The instructions for submission are in the Online Submission Folder in your course Blackboard site. The online submission is in addition to any other submission requirements that appear in this ECP.

DEFERRED EXAMS

Midsemester Exams
A student who fails to sit the midsemester examination and/or the multiple choice test at the scheduled times will be awarded a mark of zero, unless the Head of School is supplied with an Application for a Deferred Midsemester Examination with appropriate documentation satisfactorily explaining the student's absence. The application must be submitted either prior to the scheduled date of the midsemester exam, or no later than five days after the midsemester examination. The deferred midsemester examinations will take place on Saturday 12th May 2012.

All deferred midsemester examinations within the Business School will take place on this date. A timetable for the deferred midsemester examinations will be available approximately one week prior to 12th May.

This will be the only opportunity to take the deferred midsemester examination or the special multiple choice test. Any student who is granted a deferred midsemester examination and who fails to attend at the appointed time and place will be awarded a mark of zero as per the Universitys General Award Rules. The process for applying for deferred midsemester exams is as follows:

Obtain the application form from www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/forms/exams/SpecAppForm.pdf ;

Complete the application and attach all required documentation. A students application must include

if made on medical grounds, a medical certificate covering the date of the examination signed by a medical practitioner or registered nurse no later than 2 business days after the examination and stating that the student was unfit to sit the examination on the relevant day; or

if made on non-medical grounds, a statement of reasons why the student was unable to sit the examination on the relevant day and all corroborating evidence.

Submit the completed application with the attached documentation to the Student Centre at your campus.

You will receive an email to your student email account (within 3 business days) indicating whether or not your application has been approved.

Any enquiries regarding midsemester deferred examinations should be directed to the Examinations Officer, exams.officer@business.uq.edu.au .

Final Exams
A student who is unable to sit or complete a scheduled final examination must apply for a deferred examination no later than 5 days after the date the original examination was held. Applications should be lodged at a Student Centre. Faculties may approve applications for deferred examinations under the broad headings medical, compassionate or exceptional circumstances. Deferred examinations are not automatically granted. For further information (such as evidence required and time limits) please see My Advisor http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2995

SUPPLEMENTARY ASSESSMENT

Supplementary assessment is an additional opportunity to demonstrate that the learning requirements for the course have been satisfied and that the graduate attributes for the course have been attained. Supplementary assessment will be granted only where the General Award Rules and Program Rules allow.

For all UQ Business School courses supplementary assessment is weighted at 100% and covers the learning outcomes for the course. This means that the marks obtained for any progressive assessment throughout the semester are disregarded and the final result for the course is based entirely on the performance in the supplementary assessment.

The highest grade that can be received for supplementary assessment is a grade of 4.

For further information on supplementary assessment please see My Advisor http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/?page=2954

CALCULATOR POLICY

If a calculator is permitted for use in an examination in this course, the calculator must comply with the University of Queensland Calculator Scheme. Where calculators are permitted for use in the examination, students are advised to ensure that the calculator complies with the type nominated by your course coordinator. Unless the type is unrestricted, the calculator must be either a Casio FX82 series calculator, or have an 'approved label' attached which can be obtained from the Student Centre.

Please refer to myAdvisor site http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/exam-calculators for information on the use of calculators in examinations.
